name: Marble Answering Machine
short_description: A conceptual answering machine that ejects a physical marble for each voice message, which can be replayed, redirected, or used to call back by placing it in different dimples.
long_description: Designed by Durrell Bishop as a Royal College of Art student in 1992, the Marble Answering Machine represents each incoming voice message as a physical marble dispensed by the device. Placing a marble in a small dimple plays its message, setting it on a labeled dish forwards it to another person, and dropping it into the telephone's receptor calls the original caller back. Widely cited as one of the earliest tangible user interfaces, it matters as physical AI and interaction design because it demonstrates how digital information can be made graspable, persistent, and physically manipulable in everyday objects rather than confined to a screen.
ai_note: An electromechanical conceptual prototype mapping voice messages to physical marbles; it involves no AI or machine learning.

images.caption: Durrell Bishop's Marble Answering Machine, which dispenses a marble for each recorded voice message.
images.alt_text: A small desktop device with a row of marbles and dimples; each marble represents a recorded voice message that can be placed in a dent to play it back.

sources.title: Internet-of-Things answering machine from 1992, with marbles

interactions.title: A message arrives as a marble in your hand
interactions.description: When a caller leaves a voice message, the machine rolls out a single physical marble, so the count of unheard messages is something you can see and pick up rather than a number on a display.

interactions.title: Read your inbox by the row of marbles
interactions.description: The marbles line up in the order the messages came in, so a glance at the row tells you how many people called and in what sequence, without scrolling through anything.

interactions.title: Drop a marble into a dimple to hear it
interactions.description: Set a marble into a small dent on the device and it plays back the message that marble represents; lift it out and the message stays bound to that marble for later.

interactions.title: Hand a message to a housemate
interactions.description: If a message is for someone else in the house, you place its marble on a small side dish labeled with that person's name, physically routing the message to them.

interactions.title: Call someone back by placing their marble on the phone
interactions.description: The telephone has a marble receptor; drop the message's marble into it and the device dials the original caller back, so returning a call is a single physical gesture.

interactions.title: Pocket a message to deal with later
interactions.description: Because each message is a discrete object, you could carry an important marble with you and drop it into the dimple when you get home, treating a voicemail like a to-do token you physically hold onto.

interactions.title: A shared family tray of pending calls
interactions.description: Extending the labeled-dish idea, a household could keep a communal tray where unanswered marbles accumulate, making the backlog of calls to return a visible, tangible pile anyone can pick from.
